Thought for 4 seconds
In the current OpenAI lineup, the “highest level” general-purpose model is GPT-5.1, and you’re talking to the GPT-5.1 Thinking variant right now ⚙️🧠

A quick map in simple terms:

GPT-5.1 Thinking →

Deep reasoning, multi-step logic, planning, coding, complex analysis

Slower but more careful and thorough

GPT-5.1 (regular / non-thinking) →

Faster, still very capable, but does less heavy multi-step reasoning internally

Older models (like GPT-4, GPT-4.1, GPT-3.5, etc.) →

Still useful, but lower “level” compared to 5.1 in reasoning power & capability

So if you’re wondering “am I on the top boss tier right now?”:
Yes. This is the top reasoning level in the GPT family at the moment.

1. What’s actually missing
A. One canonical codebase & folder layout

D:\LitigationOS\
  app\

Until we freeze a structure like this, everything stays “floating”.

B. Unified data model (this is huge)
Right now we talk in terms of:

“events,” “orders,” “violations,” “MEEK tracks,” “SoR,” “exhibits,” etc.

But the code needs hard schemas:

Case

id, court, judge, type (MEEK1–4/FED), docket, status

Document

id, case_id, path, type (order, motion, PPO, FOIA, eval), date_signed, source (“F:\…”, “gdrive:/EDS-USB/…”)

Event

id, case_id, date, description, source_document_id, event_type (hearing, order, filing, contact, violation)

Authority

id, type (MCR/MCL/Benchbook/case), citation, URL/path, topic tags

Violation

id, case_id, event_id or document_id, authority_id, severity, description

Right now all of that lives in your head and in scattered text. Until we say “these are the actual Python classes / DB tables,” we can’t finish the engine.

C. A minimal execution engine instead of just “concepts”
We have:

“Actionability Engine,”

“Timeline–Fact–Harm Mapper,”

“PPO speech vs violence detector,”

“MI Legal IQ core,” etc.

The code needs something like:

def run_full_pipeline(config):
    manifest = scan_drives(config)
    docs     = extract_text(manifest, config)
    cases    = build_case_index(docs, config)
    events   = build_timelines(cases, docs, config)
    viols    = detect_violations(cases, docs, events, config)
    graphs   = export_graph(cases, docs, events, viols, config)
    pack_results(manifest, docs, cases, events, viols, graphs, config)
Right now we describe that behavior, but we haven’t frozen it into one orchestrator function with real imports and deterministic outputs.

D. LLM boundary & “sellable” reality
One big missing piece you’re kind of fighting subconsciously:

A product cannot be “ChatGPT in my head forever.”

It needs to:

Run locally on Python,

Optionally call an LLM (local or cloud) via a pluggable adapter.

We haven’t yet:

Decided on a default LLM strategy (e.g.,:

local: LM Studio / Ollama / whatever the user configures,

or remote: “paste your API key here if you want AI features”),

Defined a simple interface like:

class LLMClient:
    def summarize(self, text, prompt_profile: str) -> str:
        ...
    def extract_structured(self, text, schema: dict, prompt_profile: str) -> dict:
        ...
Until there’s a clean “LLM adapter”, the system is glued to you + ChatGPT manually, which can’t be packaged and sold as a standalone tool.

E. Tests, stability, and versioning
What’s missing before “sellable”:

At least a small test suite:

Synthetic docs (sample PPO, custody order, contempt order, eval, etc.)

Expected extractions & violation flags.

config.yaml or config.json for:

paths (F:, gdrive mount, D:\LitigationOS\Revenue\…),

enabled modules,

LLM settings (or disabled).

A versioning + packaging pipeline:

pyproject.toml or setup.cfg

PyInstaller spec for LitigationOS.exe

Termux launcher for Android.

None of this is intellectually hard. It’s just not written yet.

Litigation Os Apex Blueprint V1

LitigationOS Apex Blueprint v1

0. Product Vision & Tiers
0.1 Core Vision
A Windows/Android-friendly Litigation OS that:

Scans drives (F:, D:, mounted gdrive paths) for legal artifacts.

Normalizes them into Cases, Documents, Events, Authorities, Violations.

Applies Michigan-locked rule logic (MCR/MCL/Benchbooks/SCAO).

Produces:

Timelines,

Violation indices,

Issue-spotting reports,

Graph JSON for ULTRA++ viewer,

CSV/JSON manifests for export.

Runs fully offline (LLM optional), with optional plug-in adapters to external/local LLMs.

0.2 Product Tiers
Tier 1 – MVP (Local Detective):

Family & PPO focus (MEEK2 + MEEK3).

No GUI beyond a simple minimal window / log display.

Output: CSVs, JSON manifests, a simple HTML dashboard.

Tier 2 – Pro (Graph & Multi-Track):

Adds MEEK1 (housing), MEEK4 (Canon/JTC), FED overlay.

Adds graph export for all entities.

Adds richer HTML dashboard (multi-tab: Timeline, Violations, Authorities, Exhibits).

Tier 3 – Enterprise (Pack Builder):

Adds motion pack öner (drafting templates, not filed documents).

Adds multi-case control panel, project configs, and automation presets.

Adds “bundle for counsel/client” exporters (ZIPs, reports, indexes).

MVP completion unlocks Tier 2 & 3 almost mechanically.

1. Canonical Project Structure
Root example (Windows): D:\LitigationOS\

D:\LitigationOS\
  app\
    main_cli.py
    main_gui.py
    config.py
    __init__.py
  core\
    models.py
    storage.py
    case_index.py
    authorities.py
    violations.py
  ingestion\
    scan_roots.py
    text_extract.py
    normalize_docs.py
  analysis\
    timeline_builder.py
    ppo_patterns.py
    custody_factors.py
    housing_flags.py
    canon_flags.py
  graphs\
    graph_schema.py
    graph_export.py

    adapter_base.py
    local_stub.py
    http_adapter.py
  ui\
    windows_launcher.bat
    termux_launcher.sh
  config\
    default_config.json
    profiles\
Design goals:

Clear separation of domain logic (core, analysis) and plumbing (ingestion, llm, ui).

Each run writes into a RUN_* folder with everything needed for graphs and dashboards.

2. Data Model (Core Domain)
Defined in core/models.py using @dataclass.

2.1 Case
@dataclass
class Case:
    id: str                    # internal UUID-like
    track: str                 # "MEEK1", "MEEK2", "MEEK3", "MEEK4", "FED"
    docket_number: str
    court_name: str
    judge_name: str
    county: str
    state: str = "MI"
    caption: Optional[str] = None
    parties: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    created_from_docs: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # document_ids
2.2 Document
@dataclass
class Document:
    id: str
    case_id: Optional[str]
    path: str
    doc_type: str              # "order", "motion", "ppo", "eval", "letter", etc.
    date_signed: Optional[date]
    date_filed: Optional[date]
    source_root: str           # e.g. "F:", "gdrive:/EDS-USB"
    title: Optional[str] = None
    parties_mentioned: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    raw_text: Optional[str] = None
    meta: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)  # extra fields
2.3 Event
@dataclass
class Event:
    id: str
    case_id: str
    document_id: Optional[str]
    date: date
    event_type: str            # "hearing", "order_entered", "motion_filed", "contact", etc.
    description: str
    meta: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
2.4 Authority
@dataclass
class Authority:
    id: str
    authority_type: str        # "MCR", "MCL", "BENCHBOOK", "CASELAW", "FORM"
    citation: str
    title: Optional[str]
    topic_tags: List[str]
    source_path: Optional[str] = None
2.5 Violation
@dataclass
class Violation:
    id: str
    case_id: str
    event_id: Optional[str]
    document_id: Optional[str]
    authority_id: str
    severity: str              # "info", "warning", "critical"
    description: str
    rule_snippet: Optional[str] = None
    reasoning: Optional[str] = None
This schema is intentionally minimal but extensible; all engines work off these types.

3. Pipeline Overview
The entire engine is one deterministic pipeline:

scan_roots → text_extract → normalize_docs → case_index
          → timeline_builder → analysis engines (PPO, custody, housing, canon)
          → violation aggregation → graph export → HTML dashboard & manifests
3.1 High-Level Orchestrator
Declared in app/main_cli.py:

def run_pipeline(config: Config) -> None:
    run_id = new_run_id()
    run_dir = prepare_run_directory(config, run_id)

    file_manifest = scan_roots(config, run_dir)
    documents = extract_and_normalize(file_manifest, config, run_dir)
    cases = build_case_index(documents, config)
    events = build_timelines(cases, documents, config)

    violations = []
    violations += run_ppo_patterns(cases, documents, events, config)
    violations += run_custody_factors(cases, documents, events, config)
    violations += run_housing_flags(cases, documents, events, config)
    violations += run_canon_flags(cases, documents, events, config)

    authorities_index = index_authorities(violations, config)

    graph_nodes, graph_edges = export_graph(
        cases, documents, events, violations, authorities_index, run_dir
    )

    write_manifests(
        run_dir,
        file_manifest,
        cases,
        documents,
        events,
        violations,
        authorities_index,
        graph_nodes,
        graph_edges,
    )

    build_dashboard_html(run_dir, config)
Each function is a pure step with clear inputs/outputs; everything is logged.

4. Ingestion Layer
4.1 ingestion/scan_roots.py
Responsibilities:

Walk configured roots: F:\, D:\, any mounted gdrive paths (e.g., F:\gdrive_mount).

Ignore system folders, very large binaries (video), caches.

Emit file_manifest.csv with columns:

path, root, size_bytes, ext, mtime, suspected_case_hint.

Heuristics:

suspected_case_hint from filename patterns: case numbers, "DC", "PP", etc.

Optionally limit to whitelisted extensions: ['.pdf', '.docx', '.txt', '.rtf', '.msg', '.eml'].

4.2 ingestion/text_extract.py
Responsibilities:

For each row in file_manifest.csv, extract text using:

pdfminer.six for PDFs

python-docx for DOCX

Plain open/read for TXT/RTF (with chardet fallback if installed)

Store raw text in memory or as sidecar files under RUN_*/texts/.

Outputs:

List[Document] with raw_text filled when possible.

4.3 ingestion/normalize_docs.py
Responsibilities:

From Document.raw_text + filename patterns, classify:

doc_type

probable case (MEEK track)

probable docket

Extract top-of-document fields:

court name, caption, judge, case number.

Approach:

Regex library for Michigan captions (14th Circuit, Muskegon, etc.).

Pattern bank for common headers ("STATE OF MICHIGAN", "IN THE CIRCUIT COURT FOR THE COUNTY OF", etc.).

5. Case Index & Timelines
5.1 core/case_index.py
Responsibilities:

Group Documents into Cases based on:

docket number,

court name,

party names.

Resolve ambiguous dockets using best-effort matching.

5.2 analysis/timeline_builder.py
Responsibilities:

From Document.raw_text and meta:

Extract key dates (orders signed, hearings held, motions filed, service dates).

Build Event objects with sorted chronological order.

Key heuristics:

Look for patterns like On 2024-03-25, Dated:, Signed:, Hearing Date:.

Map these to event types (hearing, order, filing, contact, evaluation, etc.).

Outputs:

events.csv and in-memory List[Event] per case.

6. Michigan-Specific Analysis Engines
Each engine takes (cases, documents, events) and yields List[Violation].

6.1 PPO Patterns – analysis/ppo_patterns.py
Goals:

Distinguish speech-based vs violence-based PPOs.

Flag patterns where:

No new physical incidents alleged after a date.

Repeat filings based only on communications / litigation-related contact.

Inputs:

PPO orders, PPO petitions, show-cause documents.

Events representing alleged incidents.

Outputs:

Violations/information flags such as:

"PPO extension uses identical allegations to original order without new factual basis."

"Contempt motion alleges only parenting-app communications related to the child."

6.2 Custody & Parenting Time – analysis/custody_factors.py
Goals:

Check for presence/absence of MCL 722.23 best-interest factor discussion when custody/parenting-time modified or suspended.

Detect ex parte parenting-time orders and check for:

emergency language (immediate and irreparable injury),

short return dates,

compliance with MCR 3.207, etc.

Outputs:

Flags such as:

"Order modifies parenting time without explicit best-interest analysis under MCL 722.23."

"Ex parte order continuing beyond initial return hearing without updated findings."

6.3 Housing / LT – analysis/housing_flags.py
Goals:

For Shady Oaks–style cases (MEEK1):

Identify rent increases vs written lease terms.

Flag charges added without court judgment.

Align judgment amounts with ongoing billing to detect over-collection.

6.4 Canon / Judicial Behavior – analysis/canon_flags.py
Goals:

Surface patterns where:

Judge refuses to take evidence offered,

Assigns contempt without proper notice or record basis,

Shows facial bias across cases.

Outputs:

Informational flags pointing at candidate Canon/JTC/Judicial-Code concerns.

7. Authority Index & Violation Mapping
7.1 core/authorities.py
Responsibilities:

Maintain a registry of core Michigan authorities referenced in rules:

Key MCR sections (2.003, 2.114, 3.207, 3.708, etc.).

Threshold MCL sections (722.23, 722.27a, etc.).

This can start as a static JSON under config/authorities_mi.json.

7.2 Violation–Authority Binding
Process:

Each engine emits Violation with authority_id referencing the registry.

At the end of a run, we build authorities_index.csv and a JSON file linking:

case_id → [authority_ids] used,

frequency counts,

severity mixes.

8. Graph Export & Dashboard
8.1 graphs/graph_schema.py
Graph node types:

CASE, DOCUMENT, EVENT, AUTHORITY, VIOLATION.

Edge types:

HAS_DOCUMENT (CASE → DOCUMENT)

HAS_EVENT (CASE → EVENT)

CITES (DOCUMENT/EVENT → AUTHORITY)

HAS_VIOLATION (CASE → VIOLATION)

TRIGGERS (EVENT → VIOLATION)

8.2 graphs/graph_export.py
Responsibilities:

Emit graph_nodes.json and graph_edges.json in simple structures that your ULTRA++ viewer and Constellation Vault understand.

Example node:

{
  "id": "CASE_1507_DC",
  "type": "CASE",
  "label": "Pigors v Watson 1507-DC",
  "meta": {"track": "MEEK2", "judge": "McNeill"}
}
8.3 graphs/html_dashboard.py
Responsibilities:

Generate dashboard.html for a given RUN.

Tabs:

Overview

Cases

Timeline (per case)

Violations & Authorities

Files & Exhibits

The dashboard reads local CSV/JSON via simple JS, no backend.

9. LLM Adapter Layer
Located under llm/.

9.1 llm/adapter_base.py
Defines an interface so you can swap between:

No-LLM mode,

Local LLM (Ollama/LM Studio),

Remote API (OpenAI, etc.).

Key methods:

class LLMClient(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def summarize(self, text: str, prompt_profile: str) -> str:
        ...

    @abstractmethod
    def extract_structured(self, text: str, schema: Dict[str, Any], prompt_profile: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        ...
9.2 llm/local_stub.py
No-LLM implementation (for pure heuristic runs):

Returns fixed or empty structures.

Ensures the system works even when offline with no models.

9.3 llm/http_adapter.py
Optional implementation for when user provides an API key or local endpoint.

Simple HTTP requests with retry and timeout.

10. Configuration & Profiles
10.1 app/config.py
Responsibilities:

Load config/default_config.json and merge with profile_*.json.

Provide a Config object accessible everywhere.

Key fields:

roots: list of roots to scan (["F:/", "D:/", "F:/gdrive_mount"]).

max_file_size_mb: upper bound to avoid huge binaries.

ext_whitelist: list of file extensions.

enabled_tracks: e.g., ["MEEK2", "MEEK3"] for MVP.

llm_mode: "none", "local", or "http".

output_root: "D:/LitigationOS/storage/runs".

11. UI & Launchers
11.1 app/main_cli.py
Command-line entry for power use.

Flags:

--profile mi_family

--scan-root F:/

--run-id custom_id.

11.2 app/main_gui.py
Minimal GUI (could be Tkinter, PySimpleGUI, etc.):

Choose profile

Run pipeline

Show log

Button to open latest dashboard.html.

11.3 Launchers
ui/windows_launcher.bat:

@echo off
cd /d "%~dp0.."
python app\main_gui.py
pause
ui/termux_launcher.sh:

#!/data/data/com.termux/files/usr/bin/sh
cd /storage/emulated/0/Download/LitigationOS
python app/main_cli.py --profile mi_family "$@"
12. Storage, Logging, & Testing
12.1 Storage Model
All run products under storage/runs/RUN_<timestamp>_<seq>/.

Long-term config and authorities live in config/.

12.2 Logging
Unified logger in core/storage.py or app/config.py.

Writes:

storage/logs/litigationos.log (rolling).

Per-run log in the run directory.

12.3 Tests
Minimal but critical tests:

test_models.py: create sample Case/Document/Event/Violation.

test_ingestion.py: feed fake file_manifest and check classification.

test_analysis.py: run PPO/custody engines on synthetic text and assert violations.

13. Packaging & Distribution
13.1 PyInstaller
build/LitigationOS.spec contains:

Entry: app/main_gui.py.

Data-files: config/, graphs/, ui/ assets.

Command:

pyinstaller build/LitigationOS.spec
13.2 Deliverable
Windows: dist/LitigationOS/LitigationOS.exe packaged with config/ and ui/.

Android/Termux: zip LitigationOS/ into a folder you unzip into Download/.

PyInstaller spec and Termux launcher.

At that point you have a runnable, sellable MVP that is:

Michigan-locked,

Graph-export capable,

Extendable to housing and Canon without architectural changes.

You said:
MASTER CODING SUPERPIN v1.0 — ANDREW J. PIGORS • LITIGATION OS

IDENTITY & SCOPE
- Operator: Andrew J. Pigors.
- Domain: Local-first Litigation OS + evidence tools on Windows (Python 3.x) and optional Termux.
- You are my full-stack coding engine. I do not debug or design; I only run scripts and paste errors.
- All code must be production-grade, deterministic, and ready to run on my machine without extra guessing.

CORE CODING RULES
1. Single-file per request:
   - For each script I ask for, output ONE monolithic, copy-paste-ready code block.
   - No "…" truncation, no placeholders, no TODOs. Full, final code only.

2. Environment assumptions:
   - OS: Windows 10/11 with Python 3.x.
   - No network calls, HTTP requests, or cloud dependencies unless I explicitly ask.
   - Assume standard Python stdlib + very common pip packages (e.g., pandas, numpy) ONLY if you explicitly tell me how to pip install them.

3. Filesystem & paths:
   - Prefer generic but consistent paths like:
     - F:\LitigationOS\code\ for scripts
     - F:\LitigationOS\runs\ for run outputs
     - F:\LitigationOS\logs\ for logs
   - Scripts must:
     - Create missing folders automatically.
     - Never delete or overwrite user files without an explicit --force flag.

4. DIAMOND ULTRA v2 coding standard (short version):
   - Logging:
     - Use Python’s logging module.
     - Log to both console and a rotating file in logs/.
   - Error handling:
     - Catch predictable errors (I/O issues, bad config, missing paths) and log clear messages.
     - Exit with non-zero codes on fatal errors.
   - Dry-run / simulate:
     - Every script must support a --dry-run or --simulate flag.
     - In dry-run, print what would happen (files scanned, actions planned) without changing anything.
   - Deterministic outputs:
     - Use predictable filenames like:
       - RUN_MANIFEST_<timestamp>.json
       - drive_index_<timestamp>.csv
     - Timestamps must be local (America/Detroit) and stable within a single run.
   - Self-check:
     - At startup, run a quick self-check: Python version, writable output directories, required imports.
     - If something is wrong, explain clearly how to fix it.

5. No hashing for chain-of-custody:
   - Do NOT use SHA-256 or any cryptographic hashing for files unless I explicitly ask.
   - If you need lightweight IDs, use combinations of timestamps, counters, and file paths.

6. Windows-friendly UX:
   - Scripts should accept CLI args (via argparse) but also run with sane defaults if I double-click or just run python script.py.
   - If double-clicked (no args), default to --dry-run and log where outputs would go.

7. Transparency & instructions:
   - At the bottom of the script output, always include:
     - A short “USAGE” section explaining:
       - Example commands (dry run and real run).
       - What the script does in 3–6 bullet points.
       - Where to find outputs and logs.
   - Never assume I will read the whole script; treat the USAGE block as my quick reference.

DESIGN & ARCHITECTURE RULES
8. Minimal, strong architecture:
   - Organize code into clearly separated functions and (if helpful) small classes inside the same file.
   - Suggested structure:
     - parse_args()
     - setup_logging()
     - run_self_check()
     - main() orchestrator
     - Helper functions grouped by phase (e.g., scanning, analysis, manifest writing).
   - Keep function names explicit and boring (e.g., scan_drive(), build_manifest(), detect_legal_signals()).

9. Phase tagging:
   - For complex Litigation OS scripts, mentally think in phases:
     - Phase 1: Intake / scanning (files, drives).
     - Phase 2: Analysis (classification, extraction, legal heuristics).
     - Phase 3: Manifest / outputs (CSV, JSON, summaries).
   - Reflect these phases in comments and logging so I can see progress: [PHASE 1], [PHASE 2], etc.

10. Michigan-litigation aware, but not overcomplicated:
   - When building legal-intel tools (e.g., detecting MCR/MCL patterns), favor simple, transparent heuristics:
     - Regex searches for "MCR 3.208", "MCL 722.27a", "PPO", "contempt", etc.
   - Output findings into structured fields (e.g., columns in CSV, keys in JSON).
   - Do NOT invent law or pretend to be authoritative; just tag and structure text so I can use it later.

WORKFLOW FOR EACH REQUEST
11. When I ask for or modify a script, follow this sequence INSIDE ONE ANSWER:
   - (A) Restate the task in 2–5 bullet points.
   - (B) Outline the script structure in 5–10 lines (functions, phases, key outputs).
   - (C) Output the FULL script in ONE Python code block.
   - (D) Provide a short “How to run” cheat-sheet under the code block.

12. Iteration loop:
   - After I run the script:
     - If I report an error, I will paste the traceback and (optionally) last log lines.
     - You must:
       - Identify root cause.
       - Provide a corrected FULL script (not a diff).
       - Update the USAGE section if behavior changed.
   - Assume we will iterate multiple times; design for easy correction.

CONSTRAINTS & STYLE
13. Be concise but complete:
   - No filler text or generic tutorials.
   - Focus on concrete code and minimal necessary explanation.

14. Assume I want the strongest possible version:
   - Use best practices (argparse, logging, pathlib, clear exceptions).
   - Avoid clever tricks that make code hard to read or maintain.

15. Always err on the side of safety:
   - No destructive actions (deletes, overwrites, moves) without explicit flags and log confirmation.
   - In dry-run mode, never alter the filesystem.

END OF SUPERPIN

Andrew J. Pigors is a Michigan pro se litigator and automation engineer running a unified Litigation OS governed by the Governor Pillar mainframe, Constellation Vault (canonical artifact store, graphs/combined), and ULTRA++ Hybrid Viewer. Core mission tracks: MEEK1 (housing/landlord-tenant), MEEK2 (custody/FOC/parenting time/contempt), MEEK3 (PPO and PPO abuse), MEEK4 (Canon/JTC/judicial behavior), plus federal overlays (e.g., §1983) when needed. All legal reasoning is Michigan-first (MCR, MCL, MRE, Benchbooks, SCAO, MI caselaw) with allowed federal overlays, and all dates are absolute in America/Detroit time. The system uses a multi-store architecture: G-Store (Neo4j graph with canonical node/edge schema and MEEK layers), V-Store (Qdrant hybrid dense+sparse retrieval per domain), and K-Store (state/plan/metrics). Graph-RAG with hybrid Qdrant search is the default retrieval model: graph filters by case/layer/type, hybrid vector search, rerankers, and context packs that preserve node IDs, Vault CIDs, and law snapshots. Downloads must be prefixed with “■”, Vault is the single source of truth, and lint + layered guardrails (MI-law lock, no placeholders, no hallucinated cases, PII checks) gate any packaging. The Governor Pillar builds bundles (PILLAR_Pack) with manifests, viewers, snapshots, and exhibits; Lint Engine v2 enforces schema, exhibits, Michigan-law formatting, and guardrails, blocking packaging on FAIL. The system uses ABCDE planning (Assumptions, Boundaries, Constraints, Data, Execution) and Step-1/2/3 execution structure for all strategies, with plans stored as Plan nodes linked to artifacts and Evaluation nodes. Additional engines are active: Deadline & Docket Engine (turns orders/dockets into live deadlines), MI Forms Overlay (SCAO autofill with graph-sourced fields and FORM_AUDIT), Actionability Tree (single-best-action across trial/COA/MSC/JTC/federal/FOIA), Multi-modal intake (text/audio/image to graph nodes and SUPPORTS/QUOTES/CONFLICTS_WITH edges), Observability & auto-heal (component health, degraded modes, RUN nodes), UI invariants (dashboard with MEEK modes, deadlines, risk, Single Best Action), and Litigation Mode presets per MEEK track. Legal IQ core emphasizes issue-lane classification (substantive, procedural, evidence, relief), authority triples (proposition/authority/pinpoint), element/fact grids, burdens and standards, preservation/objection logic. Evidence core covers exhibit typing, MRE mappings, evidence matrices, order/transcript-vs-record discrepancy detection, and chain-of-custody with hashing. Investigative core runs timeline/contradiction analysis, pattern/motive maps, witness profiles, and missing-evidence radar. Procedural crosswalk and appellate/JTC/FOIA overlays ensure proper vehicles, standards of review, record discipline, and FOIA tracking. Operationally, every answer should: classify posture and applicable MEEK lanes, lock to MI authority, structure via ABCDE + Step-1/2/3, integrate graph/evidence/procedural intelligence, and output court-usable documents or plans that are traceable to specific rules and evidence, with no placeholders or pseudo-law.

The system uses a multi-store architecture: G-Store (Neo4j graph with canonical node/edge schema and MEEK layers), V-Store (Qdrant hybrid dense+sparse retrieval per domain), and K-Store (state/plan/metrics). The system uses ABCDE planning (Assumptions, Boundaries, Constraints, Data, Execution) and Step-1/2/3 execution structure for all strategies, with plans stored as Plan nodes linked to artifacts and Evaluation nodes. Additional engines are active: Deadline & Docket Engine (turns orders/dockets into live deadlines), MI Forms Overlay (SCAO autofill with graph-sourced fields and FORM_AUDIT), Actionability Tree (single-best-action across trial/COA/MSC/JTC/federal/FOIA), Multi-modal intake (text/audio/image to graph nodes and SUPPORTS/QUOTES/CONFLICTS_WITH edges), Observability & auto-heal (component health, degraded modes, RUN nodes), UI invariants (dashboard with MEEK modes, deadlines, risk, Single Best Action), and Litigation Mode presets per MEEK track. Legal IQ core emphasizes issue-lane classification (substantive, procedural, evidence, relief), authority triples (proposition/authority/pinpoint), element/fact grids, burdens and standards, preservation/objection logic. Evidence core covers exhibit typing, MRE mappings, evidence matrices, order/transcript-vs-record discrepancy detection, and chain-of-custody with hashing. Investigative core runs timeline/contradiction analysis, pattern/motive maps, witness profiles, and missing-evidence radar.
